title: Operating Systems
purpose: Understand how programs share CPU, memory, storage and devices.
outcomes: Explain Operating Systems concepts from first principles
Choose when to use and when to avoid important Operating Systems features
Implement, test and debug practical Operating Systems solutions
Answer conceptual, internal-working and production interview questions

stages.title: Fundamentals
stages.objective: Build an accurate mental model of Operating Systems before relying on frameworks or memorized answers.

topics.title: Kernel and system calls
topics.definition: Kernel and system calls is a fundamentals part of Operating Systems concerned with how engineers reason precisely about computation, resources and system behavior. It provides a vocabulary, operating model and constraints for making that part of a system deliberate rather than accidental.
topics.whyItMatters: Without understanding Kernel and system calls, a Operating Systems solution may work on the happy path but become difficult to change, debug or operate. This topic makes ownership, behavior and failure conditions explicit.
whenToUse: Use Kernel and system calls when it directly supports a required Operating Systems behavior
Use it after identifying the input, output, constraints and failure policy
Use it when the team can test and operate the resulting behavior
whenNotToUse: Do not add Kernel and system calls only because it is popular or appears in an architecture diagram
Avoid it when a simpler model satisfies the actual constraints
Avoid abstraction before the repeated problem and ownership boundary are understood
internalWorking: Input enters the Kernel and system calls boundary and is validated against its contract
Invariants and representations determine correctness, execution and complexity
State changes should preserve a named invariant and produce an observable result
Failures propagate through a defined error, retry or recovery path
tradeoffs: Correctness and clarity versus implementation effort
Runtime performance versus memory or operational complexity
Flexibility versus a smaller, easier-to-test contract
commonMistakes: Memorizing the definition of Kernel and system calls without tracing an example
Ignoring invalid input, failure and cleanup behavior
Choosing an advanced implementation before measuring the simpler one
topics.practicalExample: In the placement platform, apply Kernel and system calls to one Operating Systems workflow such as creating an assessment, loading study content or calculating results. Define the contract, implement the happy path, reject invalid input and record enough context to diagnose failure.

topics.title: Processes and threads
topics.definition: Concurrency coordinates multiple tasks whose lifetimes overlap. Parallelism executes work simultaneously; asynchronous I/O lets a task yield while waiting for external work.
topics.whyItMatters: Applications must remain responsive while handling slow networks, files, user requests and CPU work without corrupting shared state.
whenToUse: Use async I/O for many independent network or disk waits
Use bounded worker pools for controlled parallel work
Protect shared mutable state with ownership, immutability or synchronization
whenNotToUse: Adding threads to CPU work without measuring cores and overhead
Blocking an event-loop thread
Holding a lock while performing slow external I/O
internalWorking: A scheduler chooses which runnable task gets CPU time
Async runtimes register I/O readiness and resume continuations later
Threads have independent stacks but share process memory
Synchronization establishes ordering and visibility for shared state
tradeoffs: More throughput versus scheduling and coordination overhead
Shared memory is fast but increases race risk
Async code scales I/O waits but can make cancellation and error propagation harder
commonMistakes: Confusing concurrency with parallelism
Launching unbounded work
Assuming a single statement is automatically thread-safe
topics.practicalExample: Fetch independent question sets concurrently with a timeout, but limit concurrency so one user cannot exhaust database or network connections.

interviewQuestions.answer: Concurrency is about overlapping tasks; parallelism is simultaneous execution. I always design cancellation, timeout and backpressure behavior.

followUps: Which failure is most likely in a production Operating Systems implementation?

interviewQuestions.question: When should you use Capacity diagnosis?

topics.title: Processes and threads
topics.definition: Concurrency coordinates multiple tasks whose lifetimes overlap. Parallelism executes work simultaneously; asynchronous I/O lets a task yield while waiting for external work.
topics.whyItMatters: Applications must remain responsive while handling slow networks, files, user requests and CPU work without corrupting shared state.
whenToUse: Use async I/O for many independent network or disk waits
Use bounded worker pools for controlled parallel work
Protect shared mutable state with ownership, immutability or synchronization
whenNotToUse: Adding threads to CPU work without measuring cores and overhead
Blocking an event-loop thread
Holding a lock while performing slow external I/O
internalWorking: A scheduler chooses which runnable task gets CPU time
Async runtimes register I/O readiness and resume continuations later
Threads have independent stacks but share process memory
Synchronization establishes ordering and visibility for shared state
tradeoffs: More throughput versus scheduling and coordination overhead
Shared memory is fast but increases race risk
Async code scales I/O waits but can make cancellation and error propagation harder
commonMistakes: Confusing concurrency with parallelism
Launching unbounded work
Assuming a single statement is automatically thread-safe
topics.practicalExample: Fetch independent question sets concurrently with a timeout, but limit concurrency so one user cannot exhaust database or network connections.
